20: heti de autou makran apechontos eiden auton ho pater autou kai esplanchnisthe — while he was still a long way off his father saw him and was moved with compassion — esplanchnisthe is the gut-compassion verb (cf. Luke 7:13 for the widow's son; 10:33 for the Good Samaritan); it is the visceral response that precedes and motivates the action. The sequence in v.20 is rapid and movement-intensive: ran (edrame), fell on his neck (epepesen epi ton tracheton autou), kissed him (katefilesen auton) — three verbs of urgent, excessive welcome that exceed what Mediterranean honor-culture would expect of a father toward a disgraced son. The parable's God-image is defined by this excess of welcoming love.
1: ēsan de autō engizoontes pantes hoi telōnai kai hoi hamartōloi akouein autou — all the tax collectors and sinners were drawing near to him to hear him — telōnai (tax collectors — from telos = tax/toll + oneō = buy; they purchased the right to collect taxes and profited by overcharging) are regularly paired with hamartōloi (sinners) throughout Luke (5:30; 7:34; 15:1-2; 18:11); the pairing is a fixed social category in the Pharisaic worldview. Engizō (draw near — Luke's approach verb) is the movement of those coming to Jesus throughout the Gospel; it is the opposite of the Pharisees' watching distance (14:1).
2: kai diegongyzon hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis legontes — and the Pharisees and scribes were grumbling — diagongyzō (grumble, mutter — intensive compound of gongyzō = grumble/murmur, the LXX word for Israel's wilderness murmuring; dia- prefix intensifies) links the Pharisees' protest directly to the wilderness generation's murmuring against Moses (Exod 16:7-9; Num 11:1). houtos hamartōlous prosdechetai kai synesthiei autois — this man receives sinners and eats with them — prosdechomai (receive, welcome — from pros- + dechomai = receive toward oneself; to actively welcome) + synesthiō (eat with, share table — compound of sun- + esthiō): table-fellowship with sinners implies acceptance and solidarity, which is the Pharisaic complaint.
3: eipen de pros autous tēn parabolēn tautēn legōn — he told them this parable — tautēn (this — singular) before the three parables: Luke treats the lost sheep, lost coin, and prodigal son as a single parable-sequence (tēn parabolēn tautēn in the singular) responding to the single complaint of v.2. The three parables are a triptych, each intensifying the theme: a man loses a sheep (external), a woman loses a coin (domestic), a father loses a son (relational/existential). Each recovery triggers communal celebration; each celebration is the narrative icon of heaven's rejoicing.
4: tis anthrōpos ex hymōn echōn hekaton probata — what man among you having a hundred sheep — the rhetorical address tis ex hymōn (who among you) draws the listener into the scenario as the owner; the question assumes the obvious answer. Hekaton probata (a hundred sheep) represents a mid-sized flock in ancient Palestine; not a wealthy shepherd but a working-class owner for whom the loss of one is significant. katalipōn ta enenēkonta ennea — leaving the ninety-nine — katalipōn (aorist participle of kataleipō = leave behind, abandon temporarily) — the shepherd's paradox: protecting ninety-nine by leaving them to find one; the logic of disproportionate pursuit.
5: kai heurōn epitithēsin epi tous ōmous autou chairōn — and finding it he puts it on his shoulders rejoicing — epitithēsin epi tous ōmous (puts on the shoulders — ōmos = shoulder; the shepherd carries the sheep rather than driving it) is the image of complete rescue, not just location; the lost sheep is carried back because it cannot find its own way. Chairōn (rejoicing — present participle, indicating continuous joy during the return journey) — the joy begins at the moment of finding, before the communal celebration. Luke's word choice emphasizes the joy as intrinsic to the recovery itself.
6: kai elthōn eis ton oikon synkalei tous philous kai tous geitonas — and coming home he calls together friends and neighbors — the community celebration (synkaleō = call together) is the social expression of the private joy that began at finding. sugchārēte moi — rejoice with me — sunchairō (rejoice together with — compound of sun- + chairō); the invitation to shared joy reflects the honor-shame social fabric: significant events (recovery, rescue, restoration) are communal events. Hoti heuron to probaton mou to apolōlos — because I have found my lost sheep — apolōlos (perfect participle of apollymi = lost, destroyed; "the having-been-lost"); the perfect tense marks the permanent state of lostness that has now been reversed.
7: legō hymin hoti houtōs chara en tō ouranō estai — I say to you that in this way there will be joy in heaven — houtōs (in this way, similarly) draws the comparison: as the shepherd celebrated, so heaven celebrates. Chara en tō ouranō (joy in heaven — a divine-euphemism: "heaven" = God rejoicing) is the theological claim behind the parable. ē epi enenēkonta ennea dikaiois hoi ouk echousin chreian metanoias — more than over ninety-nine righteous persons who have no need of repentance — the ninety-nine "righteous" is ironic in context: the Pharisees are the self-identified righteous who consider themselves beyond needing repentance, and the parable implies their status may not be what they think.
8: ē tis gynē drachmas echousa deka ean apolesē drachmen mian — or what woman having ten silver coins if she loses one — the second parable shifts from male-outdoor (shepherd, fields) to female-domestic (woman, house). Drachma (a Greek silver coin ≈ one day's wage; the Roman denarius equivalent) — the ten-coin collection may represent a household savings or a woman's headdress ornament (coins sewn on a wedding headdress were a dowry symbol). The domestic setting (oikia = house) requires lamp and broom; the search is interior and careful rather than outdoor and ranging.
9: kai heurasa synkalei tas philas kai geitonas — and finding it she calls together the friends and neighbors — the exact communal-celebration structure mirrors the shepherd's (v.6): synkaleō (call together) + philas kai geitonas (friends and neighbors — feminine plural, the woman's social network). The three-parable sequence uses the same celebration formula for all three: shepherd (v.6), woman (v.9), father (v.23-24), building from small group to large party. Each celebration is both the parable's resolution and the icon of heaven's joy.
10: houtōs legō hymin, ginetai chara enōpion tōn angelōn tou theou — in this way I say to you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God — Luke varies the divine-euphemism: v.7 says "joy in heaven"; v.10 says "joy in the presence of the angels of God" (enōpion tōn angelōn tou theou). Enōpion (in the presence of, before) is Luke's characteristic positional word (1:15, 17; 4:7; 5:25; 16:15); "the angels of God" is a circumlocution for God's presence (the heavenly court), parallel to "heaven" in v.7. Epi heni hamartōlō metanoounti — over one sinner who repents — the singular heni (one) emphasizes that individual restoration, not mass movement, provokes divine joy.
11: anthrōpos tis eichen duo huious — a certain man had two sons — the barest narrative introduction; Luke uses anthrōpos tis (a certain man) for parables featuring a representative human figure (14:16; 16:1; 16:19). The two sons immediately set up the contrast structure: the younger who departs and returns vs the older who remains and resents. The father is the parable's central figure but is introduced only through his relation to the sons. The parable has no received title in the ancient sources; "Prodigal Son" is a Reformation-era label.
12: dos moi to epiballon meros tēs ousias — give me the share of the estate that falls to me — epiballon (falling to, belonging to — present participle of epiballō = throw upon, belong to by right; here the inheritance share that "falls upon" the younger son). Ousia (estate, property, substance — from eimi = be; "being-ness," what constitutes one's material existence) is the word for the full property being divided. In Jewish law (Deut 21:17) the younger son's share is one-third; the request is extraordinary but not without legal basis. Dieilen autois ton bion — he divided the livelihood between them — bios (life, livelihood — material sustenance, not spiritual life; contrast zoē = life) is what is divided: the living that sustains existence.
13: diaskorpisas tēn ousian autou zōn asōtōs — squandering his property living recklessly/prodigally — diaskorpizō (scatter, squander — from dia- + skorpizō = scatter; to throw in all directions, disperse completely; also used for the shepherd scattering the sheep, Matt 26:31) is the waste verb. Asōtōs (prodigally, recklessly, dissolutely — adverb from asōtos = one who cannot be saved/is past saving; from a- + sōzō = not-saving; the prodigal title derives from this word) is the English "prodigal" itself: prodigus (Latin) ≈ asōtos (Greek) = one who throws everything away, past saving by ordinary means.
14: limos ischuros kata tēn chōran ekeinēn — a severe famine came upon that region — limos ischuros (powerful/severe famine — ischuros = strong, mighty; the same word for the stronger man at 11:22) intensifies the son's destitution: his self-caused poverty is compounded by external disaster. Hyperistato (he began to fall short, to be in need — imperfect of hypereomai); the imperfect describes the onset of progressive need. Luke presents the famine not as punishment but as context that reveals the depth of the son's situation.
15: ekollēthē heni tōn politōn tēs chōras ekeinēs — he attached himself to one of the citizens of that region — kollaō (cling to, attach to, join — from kolla = glue; to glue oneself to someone, join urgently; also used for cleaving to a spouse and for the prodigal's clinging to the citizen). The citizen sends him to feed pigs (boskein choirous) — the supreme Jewish degradation: pigs are unclean animals (Lev 11:7) and pig-herding in a foreign land signals total defilement and social humiliation. The son who had an inheritance is now a pig-herder in Gentile territory.
16: epethymei chortasthēnai ek tōn keratōn hōn ēsthion hoi choiroi — he longed to fill himself from the pods that the pigs were eating — epithumeō (desire earnestly, long for — the strong desire verb, also used for the Passover longing at 22:15) + chortasthēnai (to be filled/satisfied — from chortos = fodder, grass; animal satisfaction; also the feeding miracle verb). Kerata (pods — the carob pods of the carob tree, Ceratonia siliqua; cheap pig fodder and poor-person food in antiquity) = the English "prodigal" itself; no one gave him even this. Kai oudeis edidou autō — and no one gave him anything — the social isolation is total; he cannot even receive the level of charity given to strangers.
17: eis heauton de elthōn ephē — coming to himself he said — eis heauton de elthōn (coming to himself — literally "coming into himself") is a Greek idiom for recovering one's senses, regaining awareness; it is used in Greek literature for returning to right judgment after madness or delirium. The son's repentance begins not with guilt about God but with practical calculation: how many of my father's hired servants have surplus food while I am perishing here? The movement from self-deception to self-knowledge is the beginning of return.
18: anastas poreusomai pros ton patera mou — rising I will go to my father — anastas (rising — aorist participle of anistēmi; the resurrection verb; used for arising from sleep, from a seat, and from the dead) + poreusomai (I will go — future, a firm intention). The rise-and-go combination (anastas + poreuomai) occurs at significant turning points in Luke (1:39; 10:35; 15:20). Hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou — I have sinned against heaven and before you — the double object: heaven (ouranon = God-euphemism) and before you (the father); the sin is both vertical and horizontal.
19: ouketi eimi axios klēthēnai huios sou — I am no longer worthy to be called your son — axios (worthy, deserving — the worth/merit word) in the negative: the son has forfeited the relational standing. His proposed remedy: poiēson me hōs hena tōn misthiōn sou (make me as one of your hired servants — misthios = hired worker, day-laborer; contrast doulos = slave born into the household; a hired worker is the lowest free status). The son calculates that servant-status with his father's household is better than freedom in the far country.
21: eipen de ho huios autō, Pater, hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou — the son said to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you — the son delivers his prepared speech (vv.18-19) but the father interrupts before the servant-request can be delivered. Compare v.19 and v.21: the son's scripted speech planned to say "make me a servant," but the father's action (v.22) preempts the negotiated-status request with full restoration. The incomplete speech captures the gap between the son's self-assessment and the father's response.
22: exenegkate stolin tēn prōtēn — bring out the best robe — stolē (robe, long garment — from stellō = put in order, equip; a formal or festive garment; used for the rich man's clothing at 20:46; the resurrection angels at Mark 16:5) with the superlative prōtēn (first, finest) — the best robe in the household, not a servant's garment. dote daktylion eis tēn cheira autou kai hypodēmata eis tous podas — put a ring on his hand and sandals on his feet — ring: authority and family membership (a signet ring, daktylios); sandals: free man's mark (slaves went barefoot). The three items (robe, ring, sandals) together constitute full restoration of sonship status.
23: kai pherete ton moschon ton siteuton thysate — bring the fattened calf and slaughter it — moschos (calf) + siteuton (fattened — from siteuō = fatten by grain-feeding; the most prized meat animal, kept for major celebrations). The fattened calf (reserved for special occasions, not daily meals) signals that the celebration is the highest-register festive event the household can produce. Phagontes euphrainthōmen — let us eat and be merry — euphrainō (rejoice, celebrate — the festivity verb; also vv.24, 29, 32; Luke 12:19; 16:19); the eating and rejoicing are the celebratory response to the restoration.
24: hoti houtos ho huios mou nekros ēn kai anezēsen — for this son of mine was dead and has come back to life — nekros ēn (was dead — the complete lostness is named death) + anezēsen (came back to life — aorist of anazaō = live again, revive; re-animation verb). The dead/alive + lost/found couplet (apolōlos ēn kai heurethē) is the parable's thematic core and is repeated at v.32. Kai ērxanto euphrainesathai — and they began to celebrate — the imperfect ērxanto (began) + the festivity verb: the celebration starts immediately and continues.
25: ēn de ho huios autou ho presbuteros en agrō — his older son was in the field — presbuteros (older, elder) son as the parable's second major character introduced only after the younger son's restoration is underway; the delay in introducing him makes the contrast sharp. Agros (field) is the place of faithful labor; the older son has been working while the younger was wasting his inheritance in the far country. Hōs de erchomenos ēggisen tē oikia ēkousen symphōnias kai chorōn — as he came near the house he heard music and dancing — symphōnia (music, harmony — musical performance; borrowed from Greek symphōneō = sound together) + choros (dance, dancing group).
26: proskalesmenos hena tōn paidōn epynthaneto ti an eiē tauta — calling one of the servants he asked what these things might be — proskaleomai (call to oneself, summon — Luke's call-to-oneself verb) + pyntanomai (inquire, ask — the formal inquiry verb; also Acts 23:20; Matt 2:4). The optative ti an eiē tauta (what these things might be — oblique optative in indirect question) is classical Greek syntax that Luke preserves; the older son does not know yet what has happened because no one has told him.
27: ho adelphos sou hēkei kai ethusen ho patēr sou ton moschon ton siteuton — your brother has come and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf — the servant reports two things: the brother's return (hēkei — perfect with present sense: "he is here") and the father's action (slaughter of the fattened calf). Hygiainonta auton apelabon — he received him back in good health — hygiainō (be in good health, be sound — from which hygienic; the "healthy" verb; contrast the physical and spiritual sickness implicit in the far-country life). The father's joy is expressed through the highest celebration available.
28: ōrgisthē kai ouk ēthelen eiselthein — he became angry and was not willing to go in — orgizō (become angry — the full-anger word, also 14:21). The older son's refusal to enter (ouk ēthelen eiselthein — imperfect of thelō = was not willing; sustained unwillingness, not a momentary refusal) is its own honor-challenge: refusing to participate in the father's celebration is a public dishonoring of the father. Ho de patēr autou exelthōn parekalei auton — his father came out and was pleading with him — parakaleō (plead, urge, encourage — the same word for the Holy Spirit as Paraclete) in the imperfect: sustained pleading.
29: idou tosauta etē douleuō soi kai oudepote entolēn sou parēlthon — look, so many years I have been serving you like a slave and I never transgressed your command — douleuō (serve as a slave — from doulos; the older son describes his faithful service in slave-vocabulary; he has been a faithful doulos while calling himself a son). Oudepote entolēn sou parēlthon (I never transgressed your command — parerchomai = pass by, transgress; also 11:42 for tithing while neglecting justice). Emoigde eriphon oudepote edōkas — you never gave me even a young goat — eriphos (young goat — the lowest-value festive animal; far less than the fattened calf given for his brother).
30: ho de huios sou houtos ho kataphagōn sou ton bion meta pornōn — but this son of yours who has devoured your livelihood with prostitutes — the older son does not say "my brother" but "this son of yours" (ho huios sou houtos) — the deliberate distance is the emotional core of his resentment. Kataphagōn (devoured — intensive compound of kata- + phagō = eat down, consume completely) + ton bion (the livelihood; the same bios word as v.12). Meta pornōn (with prostitutes) — the older son's accusation; the parable text at v.13 said only "reckless living" (asōtōs); "prostitutes" is the older son's interpretation, possibly projecting, possibly inferring.
31: teknon, su pantote met emou ei kai panta ta ema sa estin — child, you are always with me and everything I have is yours — the father's address teknon (child, dear child — the affectionate diminutive of family intimacy; not huie = son, but teknon = child, the intimate family word; also used in 2:48; 16:25) signals warmth rather than rebuke. Panta ta ema sa estin — everything I have is yours — the father's statement of the older son's standing: the inheritance is secure, the relationship is unbroken, the proximity (always with me) is the gift the younger son abandoned. The contrast reveals the older son's failure to receive what he already had.
32: euphranthenai de kai charēnai edei — it was necessary to celebrate and rejoice — edei (it was necessary, it had to be — the divine necessity word, also 4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 22:37; 24:7; 24:26) applied to the celebration: the father's party is not optional sentiment but necessary response to the dead-alive reversal. The closing couplet (nekros ēn kai ezēsen, apolōlos kai heurethē — was dead and lived, was lost and was found) exactly repeats the father's exclamation of v.24: both halves of the parable end with the same formula. The parable ends without resolving the older son's response — his choice remains open, as does the Pharisees'.
